title: ‘The Menu,’ ‘Bones and All,’ and 20 Movies We Missed From 2022
format: Sean and Amanda work through the pile-up of everything that came out over Thanksgiving week, then clear out a backlog of 2022 titles they never got to. Sean closes with Luca Guadagnino on Bones and All.

segments.heading: The State of the Box Office
segments.note: Glass Onion, Strange World, and a Thanksgiving-weekend crisis about what counts as a movie anymore

films.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery
films.note: Opened in 600-plus theaters over Thanksgiving as a one-week stunt before its Netflix release a month later. Made an estimated $13-15 million, a strong number for the screen count but, per Amanda, a success only because Netflix didn't have to fight for it. Neither host knows a single person who saw it in a theater.

films.title: Strange World
films.note: Disney's original animated release, on 3,000 screens over the same weekend, did poorly. Sean calls it one of the smarter Disney movies he's seen in a while but isn't covering it here; he and Charles Holmes plan an animated-2022 episode later in the year.

films.title: The Fabelmans
films.note: Spielberg's new film opened on only about 700 screens, which Sean thinks let Glass Onion eat into its Thanksgiving audience. Built for the old platform-release-then-awards-push playbook, but a 17-day theatrical window means it's already headed to PVOD in about 12 days.

segments.heading: ‘Bones and All’
segments.note: Luca Guadagnino's cannibal road movie, seen by almost nobody over Thanksgiving

films.title: Bones and All
films.note: Sean blackmailed Amanda into seeing this, by his own admission. A sensual, elevated-genre take on a cannibal story starring Taylor Russell and Timothée Chalamet, set in the 1980s and based on Camille DeAngelis's novel. Both liked it a lot: the first 30 minutes are the most physically grim, then it settles into a road movie with real Badlands DNA. Amanda calls it lovely despite going in squeamish.

segments.heading: ‘The Menu’
segments.note: a feast-film double feature partner for Bones and All

films.title: The Menu
films.note: A high-end-restaurant thriller directed by Mark Mylod, starring Anya Taylor-Joy, Nicholas Hoult, and Ralph Fiennes as the celebrity chef, with Janet McTeer, John Leguizamo, and Hong Chau rounding out the guest list. Both hosts liked it as a thriller even though its eat-the-rich messaging is broader than its restaurant-culture satire.

segments.heading: ‘Devotion’
segments.note: Glenn Powell and Jonathan Majors as Korean War wingmen

films.title: Devotion
films.note: Based on the true story of Ensign Jesse Brown, the first African American aviator to complete Navy flight training, played by Jonathan Majors opposite Glenn Powell. Sean calls it perhaps the most average movie of the year: strong chemistry between the leads and a couple of good set pieces, but familiar and undercut by a script that doesn't fully trust Majors's internal performance. Directed by JD Dillard.

films.title: Sleight
films.note: JD Dillard's Blumhouse debut, cited as Sean's introduction to the director as a very early Big Picture guest.

segments.heading: ‘All the Beauty and the Bloodshed’
segments.note: Laura Poitras's documentary on Nan Goldin, still in very limited release

films.title: All the Beauty and the Bloodshed
films.note: Laura Poitras's portrait of photographer and activist Nan Goldin, weaving her work exposing the Sackler family's role in the opioid crisis with her own history. Both hosts were struck by it; Sean says it's brushing up against his top five of the year.

films.title: Citizenfour
films.note: Cited as evidence Poitras is far from a conventional documentarian, which makes the deliberately plain opening of All the Beauty and the Bloodshed land as a choice, not a limitation.

segments.heading: ‘The Inspection’
segments.note: Elegance Bratton's autobiographical Marine boot-camp debut

films.title: The Inspection
films.note: Elegance Bratton's directorial debut, based on his own experience enlisting in the Marines as a gay man seeking structure. Jeremy Pope stars; Gabrielle Union produces and co-stars. Both hosts liked the boot-camp section but felt the film's opening and closing don't fully cohere into one arc.

films.title: Full Metal Jacket
films.note: Amanda's comparison for the basic-training stretch of The Inspection, which both hosts thought was the strongest part.

films.title: Cha Cha Real Smooth
films.note: Cited for Raul Castillo, who plays a drill-sergeant type in The Inspection alongside Bokeem Woodbine; Sean calls Castillo's turn as Dakota Johnson's boyfriend maybe the best part of that earlier film.

segments.heading: ‘EO’
segments.note: an 85-minute journey through the eyes of a donkey

films.title: EO
films.note: Jerzy Skolimowski's loose remake of Au Hasard Balthazar, following a donkey across Europe. Sean calls it sweet and beautiful, and expects it in the Best International Feature conversation at the Oscars.

films.title: Au Hasard Balthazar
films.note: Robert Bresson's film, the direct model for EO's donkey's-eye-view structure.

films.title: Cow
films.note: Andrea Arnold's documentary about a cow's life, Sean's comparison point. It shares EO's animal's-eye view but none of its winsome verve; he calls it the most punishing film he's seen in five years.

segments.heading: ‘The Son’
segments.note: Sean's strongest anti-recommendation of the episode

films.title: The Son
films.note: Florian Zeller's follow-up to The Father, starring Hugh Jackman and Laura Dern as parents of a teenager, played by Zen McGrath, going through depression and suicidal thoughts. Sean hated it: he finds it manipulative and tonally reckless about a subject he takes seriously, and thinks McGrath is miscast in a role the film barely lets him narrate.

films.title: The Father
films.note: Zeller's previous film, which won Anthony Hopkins an Oscar. Sean says his frustration with The Son clarified why The Father, which he was in the minority on, never worked for him either.

segments.heading: ‘The Wonder’
segments.note: Florence Pugh on Netflix, a soft recommend from both hosts

films.title: The Wonder
films.note: Sebastian Lelió's film, set in 1860s Ireland, about a nurse sent to observe a girl who claims to have survived without food. Both hosts single out Florence Pugh's performance and are impressed by newcomer Kíla Lord Cassidy. Amanda calls it well-made but not essential; the ending goes somewhere she found genuinely off-putting.

films.title: The Banshees of Inisherin
films.note: Amanda's comparison point, set in the same historical Ireland. She thinks it's the more accomplished script of the two.

films.title: Room
films.note: Adapted from another Emma Donoghue novel; Amanda notes The Wonder, which Donoghue co-adapted, is recognizably from the same writer.

films.title: Don't Worry Darling
films.note: Amanda's reference for Florence Pugh carrying a movie on her shoulders. She thinks The Wonder is the more effective film of the two.

films.title: Persuasion
films.note: Cited as a recent Netflix period piece the hosts didn't think worked, offered as contrast to The Wonder finding a real lane for the streamer.

films.title: A Fantastic Woman
films.note: Part of Lelió's filmography as Sean makes the case for him as an accomplished arthouse director.

films.title: Gloria
films.note: Lelió's earlier Chilean film, also cited in the filmography rundown.

films.title: Gloria Bell
films.note: Lelió's own English-language remake of Gloria.

films.title: Disobedience
films.note: Also cited among Lelió's prior films.

segments.heading: Coming Attractions
segments.note: everything still on the calendar before the end of the year

films.title: Lady Chatterley's Lover
films.note: In theaters now but not streaming on Netflix until December 2.

films.title: Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio
films.note: Not on Netflix until December 9.

films.title: Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths
films.note: In theaters now, on Netflix December 16. The hosts plan to cover it with Adam Naiman.

films.title: Babylon
films.note: Amanda dreads the runtime; Sean is thrilled by early reviews calling it overstuffed. Compares it to how he felt going in on Amsterdam.

films.title: Avatar: The Way of Water
films.note: Neither has seen it yet; Sean is already hyping the underwater sequences.

films.title: White Noise
films.note: Briefly mentioned after Sean's coverage from the New York Film Festival; a full episode planned once it hits Netflix December 30.

films.title: Piranha II: The Spawning
films.note: Sean watched James Cameron's disowned directorial debut as prep for Avatar: The Way of Water. Cameron reportedly only shot half of it.

segments.heading: 2022 Misses: The Speed Round
segments.note: a backlog of everything else from the year

films.title: Death on the Nile (2022)
films.note: Both hosts disliked it. Amanda thought the green-screen Egypt looked offensively fake, though she singles out Emma Mackey as a find.

films.title: Death on the Nile (1978)
films.note: Sean's favorite of the earlier Agatha Christie adaptations, shot on location with an all-star cast including Angela Lansbury and Bette Davis. It's the point of comparison for how artificial the 2022 remake looks.

films.title: Deep Water
films.note: Adrian Lyne's attempted erotic-thriller revival with Ana de Armas and Ben Affleck. Sean has little more to add beyond wanting whoever directed the last 30 minutes to apologize.

films.title: Uncharted
films.note: Tom Holland and Mark Wahlberg's treasure-hunter adaptation, directed by Ruben Fleischer, made $400 million worldwide. Amanda thought it looked cheap and CGI-heavy despite one strong airplane set piece.

films.title: Dog
films.note: Sean's real three-star pick: a Channing Tatum vehicle he compares to something Burt Reynolds would have made in 1978, and a reminder Tatum is a genuine movie star.

films.title: The Lost City
films.note: Sandra Bullock, Channing Tatum, and Brad Pitt's Bullet Train cameo add up to what Sean wishes had just been one movie starring all three. Amanda calls it a fun, low-stakes date movie in the Ticket to Paradise mold, and both marvel that Bullock is 58.

films.title: Downton Abbey: A New Era
films.note: Sean's second-favorite theatrical experience of the year, watched at his mother's house over the holidays. He preferred it to the first film and calls it, warmly, a very good TV movie.

films.title: Where the Crawdads Sing
films.note: Sean's most-hated film of the episode. Adapted from Delia Owens's bestselling and controversial novel, starring Daisy Edgar-Jones; he calls the murder-mystery ending a cop-out and the entire cast miscast for a story about poverty in the marsh.

films.title: Terrifier 2
films.note: Damien Leone's crowdfunded slasher sequel, centered on the killer clown Art the Clown. Sean's strongest non-recommendation for Amanda: gorier than anything he's seen in a while, though he respects it as a genuine indie box-office phenomenon that found its audience.

films.title: Speak No Evil
films.note: Sean's benchmark for how much worse Terrifier 2 will land for Amanda than this Danish horror film did.

films.title: Emily the Criminal
films.note: Aubrey Plaza plays against type in a tight 90-minute thriller about student debt. Amanda liked it and thinks it points toward a real dramatic range for Plaza.

films.title: Straight Time
films.note: Amanda's Dustin Hoffman comparison for Emily the Criminal's ex-con-gone-straight structure.

segments.heading: In Conversation
segments.note: Sean talks to Luca Guadagnino about Bones and All

films.title: Bones and All
films.note: Guadagnino traces the project back to a September 2020 call from screenwriter Dave Kajganich, and describes shooting on a monthlong scouting drive through the Midwest before production. He talks through casting Taylor Russell off Waves, working with Mark Rylance to make an unsettling character legible, and Trent Reznor and Atticus Ross's score.

films.title: Waves
films.note: The film that convinced Guadagnino to cast Taylor Russell, after being blown away once the story shifts to her character.

films.title: Suspiria
films.note: Cited as the earlier, deliberate turn toward genre that Guadagnino says people wrongly read as a surprise from him.

films.title: Call Me by Your Name
films.note: The film Guadagnino says boxed him in as 'the singer of Italian sunshine' in some critics' eyes, a framing he pushes back on.

films.title: Challengers
films.note: Guadagnino's next film, a Zendaya-led tennis comedy of manners, which he describes choosing deliberately as the opposite of Bones and All.

films.title: They Live by Night
films.note: One of three films Guadagnino had his actors watch to prepare, alongside Vagabond and A Man Escaped.

films.title: Vagabond
films.note: Agnès Varda's film, part of the same viewing assignment for Taylor Russell and Timothée Chalamet.

films.title: Jeanne Dielman, 23, quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles
films.note: Chantal Akerman's film, also part of the actors' prep list.

films.title: A Man Escaped
films.note: Robert Bresson's film, the third title on the actors' viewing list.

films.title: Beginning
films.note: Deá Kulumbegashvili's film, shot by Arseni Khachaturan, whose cinematography on it led Guadagnino to hire him for Bones and All.

films.title: Armageddon Time
films.note: Guadagnino names James Gray's film as his favorite of the year and the last great thing he's seen.

Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery

All the Beauty and the Bloodshed.title: All the Beauty and the Bloodshed

Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of T.title: BARDO, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths
